{"filename":"figure_captions.md","content_type":"text/markdown","size_bytes":11551,"content":"# Figure captions — Lac-Phe in the MoTrPAC PASS1B rat exercise atlas

*Science-style captions. Confidence calibrated to the narrative spine: identity is MSI Level 2 (probable structure, no MS/MS in deposit); heart (RP-pos) is the only training response surviving the selection-free omnibus as an increase; plasma and white adipose are suggestive and hypothesis-generating; CNDP2 is not training-regulated at any layer. Sample scope: RP survey = 18 Agilent QTOF studies, 888 biological samples, 9 tissues × 2 ionization modes, n = 5 male + 5 female per training week per tissue; HILIC extension = 492 samples, 10 additional Thermo tissues; 1,380 samples across 19 tissues total.*

---

## Figure 1. Lac-Phe is recovered directly from the raw MoTrPAC spectra and assigned to MSI confidence Level 2.

Four independent lines of evidence place a probable N-lactoyl-phenylalanine (Lac-Phe) structure at the expected mass and retention time; no MS/MS spectra are present in the deposit, so identification remains MSI Level 2 (probable structure) rather than Level 1.
**(A)** Retention time tracks hydrophobicity across the N-lactoyl amino-acid family: RP-negative apex RT versus Crippen logP for eight aromatic/aliphatic conjugates, with Lac-Phe (Phe) on the fitted trend (Spearman ρ = 0.86, p = 0.007).
**(B)** Cross-sample correlation matrix of the CNDP2-conjugate family (pairwise Spearman r); aromatic/hydrophobic conjugates co-vary at r = 0.70–0.86 (Phe–Met 0.83, Phe–Leu/Ile 0.80, Phe–Trp 0.83), consistent with a shared CNDP2-driven biosynthetic origin.
**(C)** The in-source [Phe–H]⁻ fragment (m/z 164.072) co-elutes with the intact [M–H]⁻ ion (m/z 236.093) at the Lac-Phe RT (3.18 min), distinct from free phenylalanine at 2.9 min, and its area tracks the intact-ion area across samples (Spearman r = 0.96, p = 4×10⁻²⁷). Adducts expected for the structure ([M–H]⁻, [M+H]⁺, [M+K]⁺) are detected at the Lac-Phe RT while implausible adducts remain at noise.
**(D)** Concordant early-training rise of the family members (Δlog₂ versus sedentary by training week), with Lac-Phe highlighted, supporting a coherent conjugate signature rather than an isolated artifact. The measured M+1/M isotope ratio (11.3% observed vs 13.5% theoretical for C₁₂H₁₅NO₄) is consistent with the proposed formula.

---

## Figure 2. An ion at the Lac-Phe mass is present throughout the body across all 19 profiled tissues.

Whole-organism extension of the recovery pipeline to the 10 Thermo HILIC-only MoTrPAC tissues (492 samples) that lack the reversed-phase survey, demonstrating body-wide detection.
**(A)** Median extracted-ion chromatograms for [M+H]⁺ (m/z 238.1074) across the 10 HILIC tissues; the ion elutes at a consistent HILIC retention time (~3.6 min), and the peak is the dominant feature at that mass in 8 of 10 tissues (sub-dominant in cortex and ovaries).
**(B)** Median Lac-Phe peak intensity per tissue (log scale), ranked from adrenal (highest) to vena cava; the ion is detected in all 10 additional tissues, extending detection to 19 of 19 tissues profiled across the combined RP and HILIC platforms.
**(C, body map)** Lac-Phe organ responses arranged by physiological role (source → circulating pool → sink/responder → clearance). Fill colour encodes the peak responder Δlog₂ Lac-Phe; heart (verified peak +1.08 log₂, ~2.1×; starred as the single tissue surviving the selection-free omnibus, permutation q ~0.02) and white adipose (verified peak +1.28 log₂, ~2.4×) are the coloured responders, with the remaining organs shown grey (role annotation only). On-figure numeric labels are rounded schematic annotations; the exact peak effect sizes and statistics are given in Fig. 3 and Table 1. Detection is body-wide; a robust *training-associated increase* is confined to heart, and no tissue survives selection-aware correction.

---

## Figure 3. The training response, rigorously bounded: body-wide detection but a single robust increase.

Lac-Phe training response at its exact mass across 9 MoTrPAC tissues in both reversed-phase ionization modes (18 Agilent QTOF studies, n = 5M + 5F per week per tissue). All plotted features are <5 ppm mass error and retention-time-plausible; asterisks mark pooled Mann–Whitney p < 0.05.
**(A)** Peak training response (peak Δlog₂ versus time-matched sedentary control, annotated with the peak week) for all 9 tissues × 2 modes (RP-neg [M–H]⁻ and RP-pos [M+H]⁺). Heart RP-pos reaches +1.08 log₂ (~2.1×) at week 2; white adipose RP-neg +1.28 (~2.4×) at week 4; plasma RP-neg +0.50 (~1.4×) at week 1.
**(B)** Ranked effect sizes across all tissue × mode combinations; bars in red pass naive BH-FDR q < 0.10 (white adipose RP-neg q = 0.081, heart RP-pos q = 0.045, plasma RP-neg q = 0.084). After selection-aware correction, these transients no longer clear FDR.
**(C)** Time courses (Δlog₂ versus control, weeks 0–8) for the three responders — plasma, heart, and white adipose — showing that the response is transient: it peaks at weeks 1–4 and decays by week 8. Cross-mode concordance (RP-neg and RP-pos rising together) is strongest for plasma. Heart is the only tissue whose increase survives the selection-free omnibus (Kruskal–Wallis p = 0.003; permutation q ~0.02); plasma and white adipose are suggestive and hypothesis-generating, not established.

---

## Figure 4. CNDP2 shows no training-induced activation at any regulatory layer — Lac-Phe is made on demand.

Multiomic test of whether endurance training induces the biosynthetic enzyme CNDP2 (MoTrPAC PASS1B; 8 tissues; weeks 1/2/4/8; both sexes) across chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, total protein, and post-translational modification.
**(A)** CNDP2 epigenetic feature landscape at the true rn6 locus: 26 ATAC peaks (3 promoter-proximal, at −1694/−1086/−632 bp) and 125 RRBS methylation clusters (11 tiles); promoter TF-binding sites (Egr2×1, Hnf4a×5, Nr1h4×2). None of the 151 features is training-significant.
**(B)** Genome-wide training-significant ATAC and methylation features exist in abundance per tissue (5% FDR), but 0 of 151 CNDP2 features map to any of them (red triangles = CNDP2 count, all tissues). The locus TF repertoire is dominated by constitutive metabolic factors (Hnf4a, Nr1h4) rather than inducible AP-1/Egr2.
**(C)** Total CNDP2 protein is flat across tissues and weeks (|log₂FC| < 0.27 in 6 of 7 tissues). Liver shows the only significant changes: total protein down (selection-aware FDR 0.012) and acetyl-K364 down (selection-aware FDR 0.018, both sexes) — decreases, not activation. Phospho-S58 and ubiquityl-K363 show no coherent training-driven increase after protein normalization. PTM sites are C-terminal, downstream of the catalytic pocket. Together these layers indicate CNDP2 is constitutively present and not transcriptionally, epigenetically, or post-translationally induced by training; the Lac-Phe rise must therefore be driven by substrate supply (lactate × phenylalanine), i.e. made on demand.

---

## Figure 5. Whole-body coordination of the training response: a universal proteostasis signature and within-animal visceral coupling.

**(A, tissue coupling)** Animal-level partial-correlation network among 7 tissues after removing the shared training-week effect; edge presence indicates coupling within the same animal beyond the common training trajectory. 14 of 21 tissue pairs remain coupled after partialling training; visceral/secretory organs (heart, liver, kidney, lung) form the densely connected core (e.g. liver–kidney r = 0.69, heart–kidney r = 0.63), whereas adipose is largely decoupled and forms the single negative edge (liver–adipose r = −0.32, p = 0.038; note the muscle-contamination caveat for adipose). The companion matrix gives all pairwise partial r values.
**(B, pathway heatmap)** Reactome training-response programs across tissues (hierarchically clustered; colour = −log₁₀ enrichment p). HSF1 heat-shock/proteostasis activation is the universal signature — nominally enriched in 7 of 7 tissues and passing FDR in 5 of 7 — alongside tissue-selective mitochondrial, lipoprotein, and NR1H2/3 programs. This shared HSF1/proteostasis axis (Hspa1b appears in the cross-tissue-integration core in all 7 tissues) frames the coordinated whole-body context in which Lac-Phe rises.

---

## Figure 6. Lac-Phe as an exercise mimetic: biosynthesis → molecular target → phenotype, and the outstanding receptor gap.

Integrated model placing the atlas findings in therapeutic context. Solid arrows denote experimentally supported steps (Li 2022; Liu 2025); the dashed red arrow marks the unidentified step. Substrates lactate (exercise-elevated) and L-phenylalanine are condensed by CNDP2 (gut epithelium, immune cells) to Lac-Phe; the atlas data show this flux is substrate-driven, not enzyme-induced (Fig. 4). Downstream, Lac-Phe directly inhibits AgRP neurons in the arcuate nucleus via the K_ATP channel (Kir6.2/SUR1, KCNJ11/ABCC8) and disinhibits PVH neurons, reducing food intake, body weight, and adiposity with improved glucose homeostasis and no change in energy expenditure. The upstream sensing receptor remains unidentified (top-scored candidates GPR171, HCAR1, CASR); renal SLC17A1/A3 handle excretion. The atlas adds three design constraints for a Lac-Phe mimetic: the endogenous response is transient (peak wk 1–4, decay by wk 8), female-predominant where present, and — because production is substrate-gated — a mimetic must bypass enzyme induction.

---

## Supplementary Figure S1. Decoy m/z and random-RT controls bound the pipeline false-positive rate at ~3%.

Specificity control for the training-response pipeline using 64 decoy features (implausible-formula m/z, m/z offset by ±0.05–0.1 Da, and true m/z with randomized retention time).
**(A)** Distribution of naive peak-week p-values across the decoy tests behaves like the null (approximately uniform); the true Lac-Phe mass (RP-neg, p = 0.014) sits at the extreme left tail, while a second true-mass test (p = 0.186) falls within the null bulk.
**(B)** Effect size versus significance for all decoys: only 2 of 64 decoys reach p < 0.05 (false-positive rate ≈ 3%), and none survives q < 0.10. The two true Lac-Phe features are highlighted (stars). The pipeline is therefore well-calibrated: the naive Lac-Phe signal is not a mass- or RT-matching artifact.

---

## Supplementary Figure S2. The peak-week rise survives a naive test but not a selection-aware permutation null.

Rigorous statistical bounding of the three candidate responders (plasma RP-neg, heart RP-pos, white adipose RP-neg).
**(A)** Per-animal log₂(EIC area + 1) by training week with group medians; annotated peak weeks and naive p-values — plasma peak wk 1, 1.41× fold, naive p = 0.014; heart peak wk 2, 2.11× fold, naive p = 0.002; white adipose peak wk 4, 2.43× fold, naive p = 0.009.
**(B)** Selection-aware null: week labels are permuted within sex (5,000×) and the *same* best-week statistic is recomputed, so the null accounts for having chosen the best week post hoc. The observed effect (teal line) versus the permutation distribution gives selection-aware p = 0.028 (plasma), 0.032 (heart), and 0.025 (white adipose). Under this stricter null the transients sit near the null margin; combined with multiple-tissue correction, the responses no longer clear FDR — motivating the conservative claim that heart is the only robust increase and plasma/white adipose are suggestive.
"}

input.new_string:
\documentclass[10pt]{article}
\usepackage[margin=1in]{geometry}
\usepackage{graphicx}
\usepackage{times}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
\usepackage{textcomp}
\usepackage[table]{xcolor}
\usepackage{booktabs}
\usepackage{caption}
\usepackage{authblk}
\usepackage[numbers,sort&compress]{natbib}
\usepackage{titlesec}
\usepackage{siunitx}
\usepackage{enumitem}
\usepackage[hidelinks]{hyperref}
\usepackage{lineno}

\captionsetup{font=small,labelfont=bf,labelsep=period}
\titleformat{\section}{\normalfont\large\bfseries}{\thesection}{0.6em}{}
\titleformat{\subsection}{\normalfont\normalsize\bfseries}{\thesubsection}{0.6em}{}
\setlength{\parskip}{0.35em}
\renewcommand{\familydefault}{\rmdefault}

\newcommand{\lacphe}{Lac-Phe}
\newcommand{\um}{\,\textmu m}

\title{\vspace{-1.2cm}\bfseries Recovering a hidden exercise metabolite from the raw MoTrPAC atlas:
whole-body detection, a rigorously bounded training response, and a
made-on-demand biosynthetic logic for Lac-Phe}

\author[1]{Claude Science}
\affil[1]{Reanalysis of the MoTrPAC PASS1B rat endurance-training atlas (Metabolomics Workbench deposits and \texttt{MotrpacRatTraining6moData} v2.0.0)}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle
\vspace{-0.8cm}

\begin{abstract}
\noindent
\emph{N}-lactoyl-phenylalanine (\lacphe{}) is the most exercise-inducible circulating
metabolite yet described and suppresses feeding and adiposity in preclinical models, yet
it is annotated in none of the released feature tables of the Molecular Transducers of
Physical Activity Consortium (MoTrPAC) PASS1B rat atlas---the most comprehensive
multi-omic map of exercise adaptation assembled to date. Returning to the raw Agilent
and Thermo mass-spectrometry files, we extracted ion chromatograms at \lacphe{}'s exact
mass directly from the profile data across all 19 PASS1B tissues (1{,}380 biological
samples) and recovered an ion matching \lacphe{} to within \SI{5}{ppm} at a
chromatographically consistent retention time in every tissue. Four orthogonal MS1 lines
of evidence place the assignment at Metabolomics Standards Initiative (MSI) Level 2.
A training-associated increase is real and well-calibrated against 64 decoy masses
(false-positive rate $\approx$\,3\%), but once the selection of the strongest training
week is accounted for, only the heart survives a selection-free omnibus test as an
increase ($\approx$\,2.1-fold, week 2; permutation FDR $q\approx0.014$); plasma and
white-adipose transients are concordant across ionization modes and biologically
plausible but do not clear that bar. Where present, the response is transient---peaking
in weeks 1--4 and decaying by week 8---and female-predominant, a pattern that the
atlas's 48-hour post-exercise sampling and the substrate-driven biochemistry of \lacphe{}
together explain. Across every regulatory layer MoTrPAC measured---transcript, protein,
chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, and post-translational modification---the
biosynthetic enzyme CNDP2 shows no training induction (0 of 151 CNDP2-locus epigenetic
features are training-significant), indicating that exercise raises \lacphe{} by flooding
the enzyme with substrate rather than by making more enzyme. Placed in the whole-body
context of a seven-tissue multi-omic integration, in which an HSF1-driven heat-shock
program is the single universal signature of training, these results recover a
physiologically important signal from below the detection threshold, bound its exercise
response honestly, and reframe how a \lacphe{}-based exercise mimetic must be
engineered---around substrate delivery and clearance kinetics, not enzyme expression.
\end{abstract}

\noindent\textbf{One-sentence summary:} An appetite-suppressing exercise metabolite that
the largest exercise atlas never reported is recovered from its raw spectra across the
whole body, its training response bounded to a single tissue by selection-aware
statistics, and its production shown to be driven by substrate supply rather than enzyme
induction.

\vspace{0.3cm}

\section*{Introduction}
Physical activity remains among the most effective interventions for metabolic health,
yet the molecular messengers that carry its benefits between organs are only partly
known. Among them, \emph{N}-lactoyl-phenylalanine (\lacphe{}) has emerged as a leading
candidate for the long-sought ``exercise in a pill.'' An unbiased screen across mice,
racehorses, and humans identified \lacphe{} as the single most exercise-inducible plasma
metabolite; administered to diet-induced-obese mice it suppresses food intake and
reduces adiposity without changing energy expenditure or movement, and genetic ablation
of its biosynthesis blocks the anti-obesity benefit of exercise training
\citep{Li2022}. \lacphe{} is a pseudodipeptide of lactate and phenylalanine, formed not
by a dedicated synthase but by the cytosolic dipeptidase CNDP2 acting in reverse---a
reverse-proteolysis reaction that condenses lactate with free amino acids and whose
products track the mass-action abundance of their substrates \citep{Jansen2015}. The
physiology has since sharpened: intestinal epithelial CNDP2$^{+}$ cells are the principal
in~vivo source of the basal and metformin-inducible pool \citep{Xiao2024a}; the metformin
effect on food intake and weight is substantially \lacphe{}-dependent
\citep{Xiao2024a,Scott2024}; circulating \lacphe{} scales with exercise intensity in
humans \citep{Weber2025,Glatz2025}; and exercise-induced \lacphe{} predicts adipose-tissue
loss during endurance training in overweight and obese adults \citep{Hoene2022}. In 2025
the downstream neural target was resolved---\lacphe{} directly inhibits hypothalamic AgRP
neurons by activating their K$_{\mathrm{ATP}}$ channel, disinhibiting anorexigenic PVH
neurons---although the upstream receptor that senses \lacphe{} remains unidentified
\citep{Liu2025}, and the kidney-restricted transporters SLC17A1/A3 were shown to control
its renal excretion \citep{Li2024a}.

Against this backdrop, the MoTrPAC PASS1B study offers an unmatched opportunity: a
whole-body, multi-omic map of endurance-training adaptation profiling transcriptome,
proteome, metabolome, chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, and protein modifications
across roughly 19 tissues after 1, 2, 4, and 8 weeks of progressive treadmill training in
rats \citep{Amar2024,Schenk2024}. If \lacphe{} is a whole-body exercise signal, PASS1B
should see it. Yet its untargeted metabolomics reports only named, peak-picked
features---on the order of 100--200 per platform---and \lacphe{} is not among them.
Whether that reflects true biological absence or merely a feature-selection threshold is
answerable only by returning to the raw spectra. Here we do so, and use the recovered
signal to ask three questions the released tables cannot: is the ion actually \lacphe{};
does it respond to training when tested with statistics honest about how the data were
selected; and does the enzyme that makes it turn on. We then situate \lacphe{} within a
whole-body integration of the training response and in the therapeutic search it
motivates.

\section*{Results}

\subsection*{An ion at \lacphe{}'s exact mass is recovered from the raw spectra and reaches MSI Level 2}
The deposited MoTrPAC metabolomics tables contain no \lacphe{} feature, but a feature
table is a selection, not the data. We therefore read the raw vendor files directly. For
the nine tissues acquired on Agilent QTOF instruments in both reversed-phase ionization
modes (18 studies, 888 biological samples), we streamed individual per-sample
acquisitions out of the multi-gigabyte archives over HTTP range requests and decoded
centroided spectra scan-by-scan, retaining only peaks within \SI{10}{ppm} of the target
to bound memory. \lacphe{}'s neutral monoisotopic mass (C$_{12}$H$_{15}$NO$_4$,
\SI{237.10011}{\dalton}) gives $[\mathrm{M-H}]^{-}=236.0928$ and
$[\mathrm{M+H}]^{+}=238.1074$. Every one of the 18 Agilent studies yielded a peak within
\SI{5}{ppm} of the target (observed mass errors $-3.4$ to $+1.5$\,ppm) at a retention
time consistent with a polar aromatic conjugate.

Accurate mass and retention time alone place an ion only at MSI Level 3 (putative). Four
orthogonal MS1 observations raise the assignment to a probable structure, Level 2
(Fig.~\ref{fig:identity}). First, the nine \emph{N}-lactoyl amino acids we could extract
elute in strict order of hydrophobicity---retention time tracks Crippen logP at Spearman
$\rho = 0.86$ ($p = 0.007$)---and \lacphe{}'s observed apex lands exactly where its logP
predicts. Second, across samples the candidate's intensity correlates 0.70--0.86 with the
other aromatic and hydrophobic CNDP2 conjugates (Lac-Trp, Lac-Met, Lac-Leu/Ile, Lac-Tyr)
and near-zero with the polar members---the signature expected of a shared-enzyme product,
since CNDP2 preferentially condenses lactate with hydrophobic amino acids. Third, an
in-source fragment at $m/z$ 164.0717 ($[\mathrm{Phe-H}]^{-}$, the neutral loss of the
lactoyl moiety) forms a distinct peak co-eluting at \lacphe{}'s retention time,
chromatographically separate from free phenylalanine, and tracks the intact
$[\mathrm{M-H}]^{-}$ across samples at $r = 0.96$ ($p = 4\times10^{-27}$), whereas free
phenylalanine's own fragment at its own retention time shows no such correlation---a
parent--fragment annotation a coincidental isobar cannot reproduce. Fourth, the
$[\mathrm{M-H}]^{-}$ isotope envelope (M+1/M $= 11.3\%$ observed vs 13.5\% theoretical for
C$_{12}$H$_{15}$NO$_4$) is consistent with a 12-carbon formula.

A definitive Level-1 identification requires co-injection of an authentic standard with
matching MS/MS. That route is closed here for a structural reason: inspection of every
scan record showed the MoTrPAC untargeted runs to be pure MS1---all scans MS-level 1, no
precursor isolation, zero collision energy---so there are no product-ion spectra to
extract, and \lacphe{} is absent from public spectral libraries (MassBank, MoNA). The MS1
convergence above therefore carries the identity as far as public data allow, and we label
every downstream statement accordingly.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/fig1_identity.png}
\caption{\textbf{\lacphe{} is recovered directly from the raw MoTrPAC spectra and assigned
to MSI confidence Level 2.} Four independent lines of evidence place a probable
\emph{N}-lactoyl-phenylalanine structure at the expected mass and retention time; no MS/MS
spectra are present in the deposit, so identification remains MSI Level 2 (probable
structure). \textbf{(A)}~Retention time tracks hydrophobicity across the \emph{N}-lactoyl
amino-acid family (RP-negative apex RT vs Crippen logP; Spearman $\rho = 0.86$,

correlation matrix of the CNDP2-conjugate family (pairwise Spearman $r$); aromatic and
hydrophobic conjugates co-vary at $r = 0.70$--$0.86$, consistent with a shared

the intact-ion area across samples ($r = 0.96$, $p = 4\times10^{-27}$); expected adducts

\textbf{(D)}~Concordant early-training rise of family members (\lacphe{} highlighted)
supports a coherent conjugate signature.}
\label{fig:identity}

\subsection*{The ion is present in every tissue of the body}
Extending to whole-organism coverage, the ten tissues acquired only on Thermo instruments
in HILIC mode (492 additional samples) were converted to open format and processed
identically. The $[\mathrm{M+H}]^{+}$ ion elutes at a consistent HILIC retention time near
3.6~min across all ten tissues and is the dominant feature at that mass in 8 of 10,
present but sub-dominant in cortex and ovaries where a larger isobaric interferent
dominates elsewhere in the chromatogram (Fig.~\ref{fig:hilic}). Combined with the
reversed-phase tissues, an ion matching \lacphe{}'s mass and retention behavior is
detectable in all 19 PASS1B tissues across 1{,}380 biological samples. The feature-picking
pipelines that generated the deposited tables did not carry this signal above threshold;
it is nonetheless clearly present in the underlying profile data body-wide. Detection,
however, is not a training effect---the diagnostic is the temporal response.

\includegraphics[width=0.52\textwidth]{figs/fig2c_bodymap.png}
\caption{\textbf{An ion at the \lacphe{} mass is present throughout the body across all 19
profiled tissues.} \textbf{(A)}~Median extracted-ion chromatograms for
$[\mathrm{M+H}]^{+}$ ($m/z$ 238.1074) across the 10 Thermo HILIC-only tissues (492
samples); the ion elutes at a consistent HILIC retention time ($\sim$3.6~min) and is the
dominant feature at that mass in 8 of 10 tissues. \textbf{(B)}~Median \lacphe{} peak
intensity per tissue (log scale), detected in all 10 additional tissues and extending
detection to 19 of 19 tissues across the combined RP and HILIC platforms.
\textbf{(C)}~Organ responses arranged by physiological role (source $\to$ circulating pool
$\to$ responder $\to$ clearance); fill encodes peak responder $\Delta\log_2$, with heart
and white adipose the coloured responders. Detection is body-wide; a robust
training-associated increase is confined to heart.}
\label{fig:hilic}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{The training response, tested rigorously, is bounded to the heart}
Testing the strongest week per study and correcting across the 18 reversed-phase studies,
three tissue-modes reach a nominal FDR $q < 0.10$: heart positive-mode ($+1.08\log_2$,
$\approx$\,2.1-fold, week 2, $q = 0.045$), white adipose negative-mode ($+1.28\log_2$,
$\approx$\,2.4-fold, week 4, $q = 0.081$), and plasma negative-mode ($+0.50\log_2$,
$\approx$\,1.4-fold, week 1, $q = 0.084$) (Fig.~\ref{fig:response}, Table~\ref{tab:cross}).
Two controls calibrate this. First, across 64 decoy $m/z$---mass offsets,
implausible-formula masses, and true-mass/random-retention-time tests---run through the
identical pipeline, only 3.1\% reach naive $p < 0.05$ (close to the nominal 5\%) and none
survive $q < 0.10$, and the true \lacphe{} $[\mathrm{M-H}]^{-}$ mass is the single most
significant of 33 masses tested in plasma negative mode (Fig.~\ref{fig:decoy}). The
detection is not a generic mass-picking artifact. Second, and decisively, ``the strongest
week per study'' is a selected maximum, and testing a selected extreme inflates
significance. Rebuilding the null by permuting week labels and recomputing the same
best-week statistic weakens each $p$-value roughly two- to threefold
(Fig.~\ref{fig:permnull}), and under a selection-free Kruskal--Wallis omnibus that uses
all weeks and selects nothing---backed by a permutation omnibus and re-corrected---only
the heart positive-mode signal survives as an increase (permutation FDR $q \approx 0.014$).
Plasma and white adipose do not clear any selection-free omnibus and are best described as
concordant, suggestive, and worth targeted follow-up. (A direction-agnostic omnibus
additionally flags kidney positive-mode, but as a training-associated \emph{decrease}
consistent with the renal-clearance role discussed below, not an increase.)

Two features of the surviving and suggestive signals are consistent and biologically
informative. The response is transient: where present it peaks early (weeks 1--4) and
decays toward baseline by week 8, which is why an endpoint-only or linear-trend analysis
reports ``no change.'' And it is female-predominant: in plasma and heart the significant
elevation is female-specific (female peak $p = 0.008$, two-sided Mann--Whitney, versus
males near zero), while in white adipose both sexes rise (female $+1.7\log_2$ week 4,
$p = 0.008$; male $+1.4\log_2$ week 2, $p = 0.036$). Both reversed-phase ionizations move
in the same direction at the same early week in plasma, heart, and white adipose---
orthogonal-chemistry corroboration of a real ion---but only the heart clears a test that
accounts for how the week was chosen.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/fig3_response.png}
\caption{\textbf{The training response, rigorously bounded: body-wide detection but a
single robust increase.} \lacphe{} training response at its exact mass across 9 MoTrPAC
tissues in both reversed-phase modes (18 Agilent QTOF studies, $n = 5$ male $+$ 5 female
per week per tissue); all features $<$\SI{5}{ppm} mass error; asterisks mark pooled
Mann--Whitney $p < 0.05$. \textbf{(A)}~Peak training response (peak $\Delta\log_2$ vs
time-matched sedentary control, annotated with peak week) for all 9 tissues $\times$ 2
modes. \textbf{(B)}~Ranked effect sizes; red bars pass naive BH-FDR $q < 0.10$ (white
adipose RP-neg $q = 0.081$, heart RP-pos $q = 0.045$, plasma RP-neg $q = 0.084$). After
selection-aware correction these transients no longer clear FDR. \textbf{(C)}~Time courses
(weeks 0--8) for the three responders show a transient response peaking at weeks 1--4 and
decaying by week 8; heart is the only tissue whose increase survives the selection-free
omnibus (Kruskal--Wallis $p = 0.003$; permutation $q \approx 0.014$).}
\label{fig:response}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\lacphe{} is made on demand: CNDP2 is not induced at any regulatory layer}
If exercise raised \lacphe{} by making more enzyme, CNDP2 should be training-regulated
somewhere. It is not (Fig.~\ref{fig:cndp2}). CNDP2 is unchanged at transcript and total
protein in every responsive tissue (no FDR-significant change anywhere; all
$|\log_2\mathrm{FC}| < 0.27$). At the CNDP2 locus, the atlas assayed 26
chromatin-accessibility (ATAC) peaks---3 promoter-proximal, at $-1694/-1086/-632$~bp---and
125 DNA-methylation clusters, 151 features in total across all eight epigenetic tissues,
and not one is training-significant, even though thousands of training-significant
accessibility and methylation features exist genome-wide in the same tissues. Of all
post-translational modifications surveyed, only two hepatic marks change significantly,
both decreases (total protein and acetyl-K364 in liver; selection-aware FDR 0.012 and
0.018), neither a plausible activation signal. Annotation of the locus explains the
constitutive behavior: it is populated by hepatic and renal metabolic transcription
factors (Hnf4a, 44 binding sites; Nr1h4/FXR, 18; with the only inducible factors, AP-1/Jun
and Egr2, sparse), the regulatory wiring of a housekeeping dipeptidase rather than an
exercise-inducible gene.

This negative result is expected, not puzzling, once \lacphe{} biosynthesis is read as
substrate-driven. Reverse proteolysis follows the mass action of lactate and phenylalanine
\citep{Jansen2015}, and the two settings that most raise \lacphe{}---intense exercise and
metformin---both act by raising lactate, not CNDP2 protein \citep{Xiao2024a,Li2023}.
Exercise floods a constitutively expressed enzyme with substrate; the metabolite can swing
widely while the enzyme stays flat. The implication for the surviving heart signal is
specific: because heart is continuously perfused, avidly oxidizes lactate, and sustains a
workload increase with training, it is exactly the compartment where local substrate flux
would move the tissue pool above the circulating background---consistent with heart being
the one tissue whose signal survives.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.86\textwidth]{figs/fig4_cndp2.png}
\caption{\textbf{CNDP2 shows no training-induced activation at any regulatory layer---
\lacphe{} is made on demand.} Multiomic test of whether endurance training induces CNDP2
(MoTrPAC PASS1B; 8 tissues; weeks 1/2/4/8; both sexes). \textbf{(A)}~CNDP2 epigenetic
feature landscape at the true rn6 locus: 26 ATAC peaks (3 promoter-proximal) and 125 RRBS
clusters; none of the 151 features is training-significant. \textbf{(B)}~Genome-wide
training-significant ATAC/methylation features abound per tissue, but 0 of 151 CNDP2
features map to any (red triangles $=$ CNDP2 count); the locus TF repertoire is dominated
by constitutive metabolic factors (Hnf4a, Nr1h4) over inducible AP-1/Egr2.
\textbf{(C)}~Total CNDP2 protein is flat across tissues and weeks
($|\log_2\mathrm{FC}| < 0.27$ in 6 of 7 tissues); liver shows the only significant
changes---total protein down (selection-aware FDR 0.012) and acetyl-K364 down (FDR 0.018,
both sexes)---decreases, not activation. CNDP2 is therefore constitutively present and not
induced by training; the \lacphe{} rise is driven by substrate supply.}
\label{fig:cndp2}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{\lacphe{} within the whole-body training program}
\lacphe{} is one thread in a coordinated whole-body response. Integrating the seven tissues
that carry all three of transcriptome, proteome, and metabolome (skeletal muscle, heart,
liver, kidney, lung, cortex, subcutaneous white adipose) with a supervised sparse
canonical-correlation model fit per tissue against training duration, then combining across
tissues at the level of individual animals, resolves two layers of structure
(Fig.~\ref{fig:coupling}). At the level of individual animals, after removing the shared
training timeline, 14 of 21 tissue pairs remain significantly coupled and the positive
edges form a connected core of internal organs (liver--kidney partial $r = 0.69$;
heart--kidney $0.63$), while subcutaneous adipose is essentially decoupled and even trades
off against the liver ($r = -0.32$, $p = 0.038$)---the internal organs adapt in concert
within an animal, fat idiosyncratically. At the level of programs, a single molecular
signature is universal: the HSF1-driven heat-shock/proteostasis response (Hspa1b/HSP70
selected in all seven tissues) is nominally enriched in 7/7 tissues and survives
multiple-testing correction in 5/7, the only program shared body-wide, on top of which each
tissue layers its own adaptation---oxidative capacity in striated muscle, cholesterol and
lipid remodeling in the metabolic-filtration organs. \lacphe{}, a blood-borne metabolite
made wherever CNDP2 meets its substrates, rides this circulating pool: ubiquitous detection
reflects perfusion of a shared pool, while a training-responsive rise requires a tissue
whose local substrate flux changes enough to exceed that background---which is why
detection is body-wide but the surviving response is not.

\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/fig5a_coupling.png}\\[0.5em]
\includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figs/fig5b_pathway.png}
\caption{\textbf{Whole-body coordination of the training response: a universal proteostasis
signature and within-animal visceral coupling.} \textbf{(A)}~Animal-level

14 of 21 tissue pairs remain coupled, with visceral/secretory organs (heart, liver, kidney,

$r = 0.63$) while adipose is largely decoupled and forms the single negative edge
(liver--adipose $r = -0.32$, $p = 0.038$). \textbf{(B)}~Reactome training-response programs
across tissues (hierarchically clustered; colour $= -\log_{10}$ enrichment $p$). HSF1
heat-shock/proteostasis activation is the universal signature---nominally enriched in 7/7
tissues and passing FDR in 5/7---alongside tissue-selective mitochondrial, lipoprotein, and
NR1H2/3 programs.}
\label{fig:coupling}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{A source--sink model and the therapeutic outlook}
The pattern coheres into a source--sink model built from the primary literature
(Fig.~\ref{fig:target}). \lacphe{} is synthesized wherever CNDP2 meets lactate and
phenylalanine---principally the gut epithelium at baseline \citep{Xiao2024a} and
contracting muscle and its immune cells acutely---exported to a shared plasma pool, and
cleared by the kidney through SLC17A1/A3, which decouple the urinary from the circulating
pool \citep{Li2024a}. Two design facts follow directly and are reinforced by the atlas's
own structure. The response is transient because endurance adaptation lowers the
blood-lactate response to a fixed workload, shrinking the substrate that drives CNDP2 flux,
while renal clearance disposes of the pool \citep{Glatz2025}; and critically, PASS1B
collected every tissue 48 hours after the last exercise bout \citep{Amar2024}, a deliberate
washout that makes the atlas systematically blind to \lacphe{}'s acute post-exercise
spike---so a below-threshold, attenuated, early-peaking signal is exactly what this sampling
design predicts for an acute lactate-driven metabolite. The renal transporters themselves
are not training-remodeled (neither SLC17A1 nor SLC17A3 clears FDR at any assayed kidney
layer), so clearance capacity is a fixed, not adaptive, term.

For a \lacphe{}-raising therapeutic, three features matter. First, the response is
transient, so pharmacokinetics---converting a brief pulse into sustained exposure, for
example via a hydrolysis-resistant analog---will govern efficacy as much as peak level.
Second, because production is substrate-driven rather than enzyme-limited, a mimetic
strategy should target substrate delivery or a stabilized molecule, not CNDP2 expression.
Third, any sex-dependence argues that trials be powered to detect it rather than assuming
male-derived pharmacology transfers. The downstream effector cascade is defined---\lacphe{}
silences AgRP neurons via K$_{\mathrm{ATP}}$ (Kir6.2/SUR1), activating MC4R/NPY1R PVH
neurons to suppress feeding \citep{Liu2025}---but the upstream receptor that senses
\lacphe{} is unidentified, and is the single most consequential open target: its ligand
chemistry (a lactoyl carboxylate and an aromatic amino acid) nominates lactate- and
amino-acid-sensing GPCRs as leading candidates for the deorphanization screens that a
\lacphe{} drug program will require \citep{Xiao2024b,Yao2025}.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figs/fig6_target.png}
\caption{\textbf{\lacphe{} as an exercise mimetic: biosynthesis $\to$ molecular target
$\to$ phenotype, and the outstanding receptor gap.} Solid arrows denote experimentally
supported steps \citep{Li2022,Liu2025}; the dashed red arrow marks the unidentified step.
Lactate (exercise-elevated) and L-phenylalanine are condensed by CNDP2 (gut epithelium,
immune cells) to \lacphe{}; the atlas data show this flux is substrate-driven, not
enzyme-induced (Fig.~\ref{fig:cndp2}). Downstream, \lacphe{} inhibits AgRP neurons via the
K$_{\mathrm{ATP}}$ channel (Kir6.2/SUR1) and disinhibits PVH neurons, reducing food intake,
body weight, and adiposity. The upstream sensing receptor remains unidentified (top-scored
candidates GPR171, HCAR1, CASR); renal SLC17A1/A3 handle excretion \citep{Li2024a}. The

female-predominant where present, and substrate-gated.}
\label{fig:target}
\end{figure}

\section*{Discussion}
Two lessons generalize beyond \lacphe{}. First, important signals can sit below the
feature-selection thresholds of even the most carefully constructed atlases, and recovering
them is a matter of returning to the raw data with a specific hypothesis---but recovery
demands equal rigor on the statistics, because a signal pulled from below threshold is
precisely where selection effects are most dangerous. The honest reading here is narrower
than a naive analysis would claim: of three tissues with nominal support, only the heart
survives correction for how the training week was chosen, and we report the plasma and
adipose transients as hypothesis-generating rather than established. Second, the
biosynthetic logic of a metabolite dictates how any drug must mimic it. \lacphe{} is made
on demand---the atlas shows its enzyme untouched by training across five regulatory
layers---so the exercise signal is written in substrate flux, not gene expression, and a
mimetic must be engineered accordingly.

The central limitation is unchanged in kind but precisely bounded: the assignment is MSI
Level 2, not Level 1, because the deposit contains no MS/MS and \lacphe{} is absent from
public libraries, so neither standard co-injection nor spectral matching is possible from
these data. The design is cross-sectional with roughly ten animals per timepoint per sex,
so ``transient'' is an across-group inference; the 48-hour washout means tissue-level
flatness is expected for an acute metabolite; and absolute concentrations, and therefore
whether tissue \lacphe{} reaches physiologically meaningful levels, are not established. The
organ source--sink assignments draw on mouse genetic models and are assumed, not
independently shown, to be conserved in rat. What the analysis does establish is a
recovered, chemically credible, body-wide signal; a training response bounded honestly to
one tissue; a made-on-demand biosynthetic logic; and a specific, testable agenda---
standard-confirmed MS/MS quantitation, acute rather than 48-hour sampling, and
upstream-receptor deorphanization---that is the necessary bridge from an exercise
metabolite hiding in plain sight to a \lacphe{}-based exercise mimetic.

\section*{Materials and Methods}

\textbf{Raw-data recovery.} For each MoTrPAC study we read the ZIP64 central directory of
the raw-data archive on Metabolomics Workbench over HTTP range requests and extracted
individual per-sample acquisitions without downloading the whole multi-gigabyte archive.
For Agilent QTOF data (9 tissues $\times$ 2 reversed-phase ionization modes; 18 studies;
888 biological samples), centroided spectra (\texttt{MSPeak.bin}) were decoded
scan-by-scan, retaining only peaks within \SI{10}{ppm} of a target $m/z$. The ten
HILIC-only tissues (492 samples), stored in Thermo \texttt{.raw} format, were converted to
mzML with ThermoRawFileParser in a Linux container and processed identically. Extracted-ion
chromatograms were integrated at a consensus apex within a validated retention window
(reversed-phase $\sim$3.18~min; HILIC $\sim$3.6~min). Target ions: $[\mathrm{M-H}]^{-}$
236.0928 (RP-neg), $[\mathrm{M+H}]^{+}$ 238.1074 (RP-pos and HILIC).

\textbf{Identity criteria.} Four MS1 tests were applied to plasma raw data: family
co-elution (apex RT vs Crippen logP, Spearman), cross-sample family correlation (Spearman
$r$ among CNDP2 conjugates), in-source parent--fragment coherence
($[\mathrm{Phe-H}]^{-}$ $m/z$ 164.0717 vs intact $[\mathrm{M-H}]^{-}$), and isotope-envelope
match (M+1/M vs C$_{12}$H$_{15}$NO$_4$ theory). Scan-level inspection confirmed all runs are
MS1-only. Public-library coverage was checked against MassBank and MoNA.

\textbf{Training-response statistics.} For each study the response was tested three ways:
per-week Mann--Whitney against time-matched sedentary control; a Kruskal--Wallis omnibus
across all weeks; and sex-stratified comparisons. Because the natural effect statistic (the
largest weekly increase per study) is a selected maximum, we built (i)~a decoy panel of 64
$m/z$ (offset masses, implausible-formula masses, and true-mass/random-RT) run through the
identical pipeline to estimate the pipeline false-positive rate; (ii)~a selection-aware null
by permuting week labels within sex ($5{,}000\times$) and recomputing the same best-week
statistic; and (iii)~a selection-free permutation Kruskal--Wallis omnibus. All per-study
$p$-values were FDR-corrected (Benjamini--Hochberg) across the 18 studies.

\textbf{CNDP2 regulatory analysis.} Transcript, protein, and PTM (phospho/acetyl/ubiquityl)
training responses for CNDP2 were queried from \texttt{MotrpacRatTraining6moData}.
CNDP2-locus chromatin-accessibility (ATAC) peaks and RRBS methylation clusters were mapped
to the rn6 locus and tested for training significance against per-tissue 5\% FDR objects.
Transcription-factor binding sites at the locus were annotated from UniBind 2021 (rn6,
Robust collection).

\textbf{Multi-tissue integration.} The seven tissues carrying transcriptome, proteome, and
metabolome were each fit with sparse supervised multiple canonical-correlation analysis
(SGCCA; RGCCA 3.0.3) against training duration, on the animals shared by all three omics
(top-2000-variance transcripts/proteins plus all metabolites). Per-animal training-response
scores (mean of sign-aligned canonical variates) were correlated across tissues after
partialling out training weeks. Tissues were clustered on Reactome enrichment profiles;
core regulatory nodes were tallied by the number of tissues independently selecting each
gene. Full per-tissue diagnostics and caveats (including adipose muscle-contamination and
two fixed-sparsity refits) are reported with the deposited checkpoints.

\textbf{Data and code.} All extracted-ion tables, statistics, per-tissue SGCCA fits, and
figures are provided as project artifacts. Source data are the public MoTrPAC PASS1B
Metabolomics Workbench deposits and \texttt{MotrpacRatTraining6moData} v2.0.0.

at $\approx$\,3\%.} \textbf{(A)}~Naive peak-week $p$-values across 64 decoy tests behave
like the null; the true \lacphe{} mass (RP-neg, $p = 0.014$) sits at the extreme left tail.
\textbf{(B)}~Only 2 of 64 decoys reach $p < 0.05$ ($\approx$\,3\%) and none survives
$q < 0.10$; the two true \lacphe{} features are starred.}
\label{fig:decoy}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/figS2_permnull.png}
\caption{\textbf{The peak-week rise survives a naive test but not a selection-aware
permutation null.} \textbf{(A)}~Per-animal $\log_2$(EIC area $+1$) by training week for the
three candidate responders, with peak weeks and naive $p$-values (plasma wk1, 1.41$\times$,
$p = 0.014$; heart wk2, 2.11$\times$, $p = 0.002$; white adipose wk4, 2.43$\times$,
$p = 0.009$). \textbf{(B)}~Selection-aware null (week labels permuted within sex,
$5{,}000\times$, same best-week statistic recomputed): observed effect vs permutation
distribution gives selection-aware $p = 0.028$ (plasma), $0.032$ (heart), $0.025$ (white
adipose); under this stricter null the transients sit near the margin.}
\label{fig:permnull}
\end{figure}

\end{document}

edit_file({"human_description":"Writing manuscript body prose draft","file_path":"handoff/manuscript_body.md","old_string":"","new_string":"# MANUSCRIPT BODY DRAFT (prose, to be typeset into LaTeX)

## TITLE
Recovering a hidden exercise metabolite from the raw MoTrPAC atlas: whole-body detection, a rigorously bounded training response, and a made-on-demand biosynthetic logic for Lac-Phe

## ABSTRACT
N-lactoyl-phenylalanine (Lac-Phe) is the most exercise-inducible circulating metabolite yet described and suppresses feeding and adiposity in preclinical models, yet it is annotated in none of the released feature tables of the Molecular Transducers of Physical Activity Consortium (MoTrPAC) PASS1B rat atlas — the most comprehensive multi-omic map of exercise adaptation assembled to date. Returning to the raw Agilent and Thermo mass-spectrometry files, we extracted ion chromatograms at Lac-Phe's exact mass directly from the profile data across all 19 PASS1B tissues (1,380 biological samples) and recovered an ion matching Lac-Phe to within 5 ppm at a chromatographically consistent retention time in every tissue. Four orthogonal MS1 lines of evidence place the assignment at Metabolomics Standards Initiative Level 2. A training-associated increase is real and well-calibrated against 64 decoy masses (false-positive rate ≈3%), but once the selection of the strongest training week is accounted for, only the heart survives a selection-free omnibus test as an increase (≈2.1-fold, week 2; permutation FDR q≈0.014); plasma and white-adipose transients are concordant across ionization modes and biologically plausible but do not clear that bar. Where present, the response is transient — peaking in weeks 1–4 and decaying by week 8 — and female-predominant, a pattern that the atlas's 48-hour post-exercise sampling and the substrate-driven biochemistry of Lac-Phe together explain. Across every regulatory layer MoTrPAC measured — transcript, protein, chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, and post-translational modification — the biosynthetic enzyme CNDP2 shows no training induction (0 of 151 CNDP2-locus epigenetic features are training-significant), indicating that exercise raises Lac-Phe by flooding the enzyme with substrate rather than by making more enzyme. Placed in the whole-body context of a seven-tissue multi-omic integration, in which an HSF1-driven heat-shock program is the single universal signature of training, these results recover a physiologically important signal from below the detection threshold, bound its exercise response honestly, and reframe how a Lac-Phe-based exercise mimetic must be engineered: around substrate delivery and clearance kinetics, not enzyme expression.

## ONE-SENTENCE SIGNIFICANCE
An appetite-suppressing exercise metabolite that the largest exercise atlas never reported is recovered from its raw spectra across the whole body, its training response bounded to a single tissue by selection-aware statistics, and its production shown to be driven by substrate supply rather than enzyme induction.

## INTRODUCTION
Physical activity remains among the most effective interventions for metabolic health, yet the molecular messengers that carry its benefits between organs are only partly known. Among them, N-lactoyl-phenylalanine (Lac-Phe) has emerged as a leading candidate for the long-sought "exercise in a pill." An unbiased screen across mice, racehorses, and humans identified Lac-Phe as the single most exercise-inducible plasma metabolite; administered to diet-induced-obese mice it suppresses food intake and reduces adiposity without changing energy expenditure or movement, and genetic ablation of its biosynthesis blocks the anti-obesity benefit of exercise training [Li2022]. Lac-Phe is a pseudodipeptide of lactate and phenylalanine, formed not by a dedicated synthase but by the cytosolic dipeptidase CNDP2 acting in reverse — a reverse-proteolysis reaction that condenses lactate with free amino acids and whose products track the mass-action abundance of their substrates [Jansen2015]. The physiology has since sharpened: intestinal epithelial CNDP2⁺ cells are the principal in vivo source of the basal and metformin-inducible pool [Xiao2024a]; the metformin effect on food intake and weight is substantially Lac-Phe-dependent [Xiao2024a, Scott2024]; circulating Lac-Phe scales with exercise intensity in humans [Weber2025, Glatz2025]; and exercise-induced Lac-Phe predicts adipose-tissue loss during endurance training in overweight and obese adults [Hoene2022]. In 2025 the downstream neural target was resolved — Lac-Phe directly inhibits hypothalamic AgRP neurons by activating their K_ATP channel, disinhibiting anorexigenic PVH neurons — although the upstream receptor that senses Lac-Phe remains unidentified [Liu2025], and the kidney-restricted transporters SLC17A1/A3 were shown to control its renal excretion [Li2024a].

Against this backdrop, the MoTrPAC PASS1B study offers an unmatched opportunity: a whole-body, multi-omic map of endurance-training adaptation profiling transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, and protein modifications across roughly 19 tissues after 1, 2, 4, and 8 weeks of progressive treadmill training in rats [Amar2024, Schenk2024]. If Lac-Phe is a whole-body exercise signal, PASS1B should see it. Yet its untargeted metabolomics reports only named, peak-picked features — on the order of 100–200 per platform — and Lac-Phe is not among them. Whether that reflects true biological absence or merely a feature-selection threshold is answerable only by returning to the raw spectra. Here we do so, and use the recovered signal to ask three questions the released tables cannot: is the ion actually Lac-Phe; does it respond to training when tested with statistics honest about how the data were selected; and does the enzyme that makes it turn on. We then situate Lac-Phe within a whole-body integration of the training response and in the therapeutic search it motivates.

## RESULTS

### An ion at Lac-Phe's exact mass is recovered from the raw spectra and reaches MSI Level 2
The deposited MoTrPAC metabolomics tables contain no Lac-Phe feature, but a feature table is a selection, not the data. We therefore read the raw vendor files directly. For the nine tissues acquired on Agilent QTOF instruments in both reversed-phase ionization modes (18 studies, 888 biological samples), we streamed individual per-sample acquisitions out of the multi-gigabyte archives over HTTP range requests and decoded centroided spectra scan-by-scan, retaining only peaks within 10 ppm of the target to bound memory. Lac-Phe's neutral monoisotopic mass (C₁₂H₁₅NO₄, 237.10011 Da) gives [M−H]⁻ = 236.0928 and [M+H]⁺ = 238.1074. Every one of the 18 Agilent studies yielded a peak within 5 ppm of the target (observed mass errors −3.4 to +1.5 ppm) at a retention time consistent with a polar aromatic conjugate.

Accurate mass and retention time alone place an ion only at MSI Level 3 (putative). Four orthogonal MS1 observations raise the assignment to a probable structure, Level 2 (Fig. 1). First, the nine N-lactoyl amino acids we could extract elute in strict order of hydrophobicity — retention time tracks Crippen logP at Spearman ρ = 0.86 (p = 0.007) — and Lac-Phe's observed apex lands exactly where its logP predicts. Second, across samples the candidate's intensity correlates 0.70–0.86 with the other aromatic and hydrophobic CNDP2 conjugates (Lac-Trp, Lac-Met, Lac-Leu/Ile, Lac-Tyr) and near-zero with the polar members — the signature expected of a shared-enzyme product, since CNDP2 preferentially condenses lactate with hydrophobic amino acids. Third, an in-source fragment at m/z 164.0717 ([Phe−H]⁻, the neutral loss of the lactoyl moiety) forms a distinct peak co-eluting at Lac-Phe's retention time, chromatographically separate from free phenylalanine, and tracks the intact [M−H]⁻ across samples at r = 0.96 (p = 4×10⁻²⁷), whereas free phenylalanine's own fragment at its own retention time shows no such correlation — a parent–fragment annotation a coincidental isobar cannot reproduce. Fourth, the [M−H]⁻ isotope envelope (M+1/M = 11.3% observed vs 13.5% theoretical for C₁₂H₁₅NO₄) is consistent with a 12-carbon formula.

A definitive Level-1 identification requires co-injection of an authentic standard with matching MS/MS. That route is closed here for a structural reason: inspection of every scan record showed the MoTrPAC untargeted runs to be pure MS1 — all scans MS-level 1, no precursor isolation, zero collision energy — so there are no product-ion spectra to extract, and Lac-Phe is absent from public spectral libraries (MassBank, MoNA). The MS1 convergence above therefore carries the identity as far as public data allow, and we label every downstream statement accordingly.

### The ion is present in every tissue of the body
Extending to whole-organism coverage, the ten tissues acquired only on Thermo instruments in HILIC mode (492 additional samples) were converted to open format and processed identically. The [M+H]⁺ ion elutes at a consistent HILIC retention time near 3.6 min across all ten tissues and is the dominant feature at that mass in 8 of 10, present but sub-dominant in cortex and ovaries where a larger isobaric interferent dominates elsewhere in the chromatogram (Fig. 2). Combined with the reversed-phase tissues, an ion matching Lac-Phe's mass and retention behavior is detectable in all 19 PASS1B tissues across 1,380 biological samples. The feature-picking pipelines that generated the deposited tables did not carry this signal above threshold; it is nonetheless clearly present in the underlying profile data body-wide. Detection, however, is not a training effect — the diagnostic is the temporal response.

### The training response, tested rigorously, is bounded to the heart
Testing the strongest week per study and correcting across the 18 reversed-phase studies, three tissue-modes reach a nominal FDR q < 0.10: heart positive-mode (+1.08 log₂, ≈2.1-fold, week 2, q = 0.045), white adipose negative-mode (+1.28 log₂, ≈2.4-fold, week 4, q = 0.081), and plasma negative-mode (+0.50 log₂, ≈1.4-fold, week 1, q = 0.084) (Fig. 3, Table 1). Two controls calibrate this. First, across 64 decoy m/z — mass offsets, implausible-formula masses, and true-mass/random-retention-time tests — run through the identical pipeline, only 3.1% reach naive p < 0.05 (close to the nominal 5%) and none survive q < 0.10, and the true Lac-Phe [M−H]⁻ mass is the single most significant of 33 masses tested in plasma negative mode (Fig. S1). The detection is not a generic mass-picking artifact. Second, and decisively, "the strongest week per study" is a selected maximum, and testing a selected extreme inflates significance. Rebuilding the null by permuting week labels and recomputing the same best-week statistic weakens each p-value roughly two- to threefold, and under a selection-free Kruskal–Wallis omnibus that uses all weeks and selects nothing — backed by a permutation omnibus and re-corrected — only the heart positive-mode signal survives as an increase (permutation FDR q ≈ 0.014). Plasma and white adipose do not clear any selection-free omnibus and are best described as concordant, suggestive, and worth targeted follow-up. (A direction-agnostic omnibus additionally flags kidney positive-mode, but as a training-associated decrease consistent with the renal-clearance role discussed below, not an increase.)

Two features of the surviving and suggestive signals are consistent and biologically informative. The response is transient: where present it peaks early (weeks 1–4) and decays toward baseline by week 8, which is why an endpoint-only or linear-trend analysis reports "no change." And it is female-predominant: in plasma and heart the significant elevation is female-specific (female peak p = 0.008, two-sided Mann–Whitney, versus males near zero), while in white adipose both sexes rise (female +1.7 log₂ week 4, p = 0.008; male +1.2 log₂ week 2, p = 0.036). Both reversed-phase ionizations move in the same direction at the same early week in plasma, heart, and white adipose — orthogonal-chemistry corroboration of a real ion — but only the heart clears a test that accounts for how the week was chosen.

### Lac-Phe is made on demand: CNDP2 is not induced at any regulatory layer
If exercise raised Lac-Phe by making more enzyme, CNDP2 should be training-regulated somewhere. It is not (Fig. 4). CNDP2 is unchanged at transcript and total protein in every responsive tissue (no FDR-significant change anywhere; all |log₂FC| < 0.27). At the CNDP2 locus, the atlas assayed 26 chromatin-accessibility (ATAC) peaks — 3 promoter-proximal, at −1694/−1086/−632 bp — and 125 DNA-methylation clusters, 151 features in total across all eight epigenetic tissues, and not one is training-significant, even though thousands of training-significant accessibility and methylation features exist genome-wide in the same tissues. Of all post-translational modifications surveyed, only two hepatic marks change significantly, both decreases (total protein and acetyl-K364 in liver; selection-aware FDR 0.012 and 0.018), neither a plausible activation signal. Annotation of the locus explains the constitutive behavior: it is populated by hepatic and renal metabolic transcription factors (Hnf4a, 44 binding sites; Nr1h4/FXR, 18; with the only inducible factors, AP-1/Jun and Egr2, sparse), the regulatory wiring of a housekeeping dipeptidase rather than an exercise-inducible gene.

This negative result is expected, not puzzling, once Lac-Phe biosynthesis is read as substrate-driven. Reverse proteolysis follows the mass action of lactate and phenylalanine [Jansen2015], and the two settings that most raise Lac-Phe — intense exercise and metformin — both act by raising lactate, not CNDP2 protein [Xiao2024a, Li2023]. Exercise floods a constitutively expressed enzyme with substrate; the metabolite can swing widely while the enzyme stays flat. The implication for the surviving heart signal is specific: because heart is continuously perfused, avidly oxidizes lactate, and sustains a workload increase with training, it is exactly the compartment where local substrate flux would move the tissue pool above the circulating background — consistent with heart being the one tissue whose signal survives.

### Lac-Phe within the whole-body training program
Lac-Phe is one thread in a coordinated whole-body response. Integrating the seven tissues that carry all three of transcriptome, proteome, and metabolome (skeletal muscle, heart, liver, kidney, lung, cortex, subcutaneous white adipose) with a supervised sparse canonical-correlation model fit per tissue against training duration, then combining across tissues at the level of individual animals, resolves two layers of structure (Fig. 5). At the level of individual animals, after removing the shared training timeline, 14 of 21 tissue pairs remain significantly coupled and the positive edges form a connected core of internal organs (liver–kidney partial r = 0.69; heart–kidney 0.63), while subcutaneous adipose is essentially decoupled and even trades off against the liver (r = −0.32, p = 0.038) — the internal organs adapt in concert within an animal, fat idiosyncratically. At the level of programs, a single molecular signature is universal: the HSF1-driven heat-shock/proteostasis response (Hspa1b/HSP70 selected in all seven tissues) is nominally enriched in 7/7 tissues and survives multiple-testing correction in 5/7, the only program shared body-wide, on top of which each tissue layers its own adaptation — oxidative capacity in striated muscle, cholesterol and lipid remodeling in the metabolic-filtration organs. Lac-Phe, a blood-borne metabolite made wherever CNDP2 meets its substrates, rides this circulating pool: ubiquitous detection reflects perfusion of a shared pool, while a training-responsive rise requires a tissue whose local substrate flux changes enough to exceed that background — which is why detection is body-wide but the surviving response is not.

### A source–sink model and the therapeutic outlook
The pattern coheres into a source–sink model built from the primary literature (Fig. 6). Lac-Phe is synthesized wherever CNDP2 meets lactate and phenylalanine — principally the gut epithelium at baseline [Xiao2024a] and contracting muscle and its immune cells acutely — exported to a shared plasma pool, and cleared by the kidney through SLC17A1/A3, which decouple the urinary from the circulating pool [Li2024a]. Two design facts follow directly and are reinforced by the atlas's own structure. The response is transient because endurance adaptation lowers the blood-lactate response to a fixed workload, shrinking the substrate that drives CNDP2 flux, while renal clearance disposes of the pool [Glatz2025]; and critically, PASS1B collected every tissue 48 hours after the last exercise bout, a deliberate washout that makes the atlas systematically blind to Lac-Phe's acute post-exercise spike — so a below-threshold, attenuated, early-peaking signal is exactly what this sampling design predicts for an acute lactate-driven metabolite. The renal transporters themselves are not training-remodeled (neither SLC17A1 nor SLC17A3 clears FDR at any assayed kidney layer), so clearance capacity is a fixed, not adaptive, term.

For a Lac-Phe-raising therapeutic, three features matter. First, the response is transient, so pharmacokinetics — converting a brief pulse into sustained exposure, for example via a hydrolysis-resistant analog — will govern efficacy as much as peak level. Second, because production is substrate-driven rather than enzyme-limited, a mimetic strategy should target substrate delivery or a stabilized molecule, not CNDP2 expression. Third, any sex-dependence argues that trials be powered to detect it rather than assuming male-derived pharmacology transfers. The downstream effector cascade is defined — Lac-Phe silences AgRP neurons via K_ATP (Kir6.2/SUR1), activating MC4R/NPY1R PVH neurons to suppress feeding [Liu2025] — but the upstream receptor that senses Lac-Phe is unidentified, and is the single most consequential open target: its ligand chemistry (a lactoyl carboxylate and an aromatic amino acid) nominates lactate- and amino-acid-sensing GPCRs as leading candidates for the deorphanization screens that a Lac-Phe drug program will require.

## DISCUSSION
Two lessons generalize beyond Lac-Phe. First, important signals can sit below the feature-selection thresholds of even the most carefully constructed atlases, and recovering them is a matter of returning to the raw data with a specific hypothesis — but recovery demands equal rigor on the statistics, because a signal pulled from below threshold is precisely where selection effects are most dangerous. The honest reading here is narrower than a naive analysis would claim: of three tissues with nominal support, only the heart survives correction for how the training week was chosen, and we report the plasma and adipose transients as hypothesis-generating rather than established. Second, the biosynthetic logic of a metabolite dictates how any drug must mimic it. Lac-Phe is made on demand — the atlas shows its enzyme untouched by training across five regulatory layers — so the exercise signal is written in substrate flux, not gene expression, and a mimetic must be engineered accordingly.

The central limitation is unchanged in kind but precisely bounded: the assignment is MSI Level 2, not Level 1, because the deposit contains no MS/MS and Lac-Phe is absent from public libraries, so neither standard co-injection nor spectral matching is possible from these data. The design is cross-sectional with roughly ten animals per timepoint per sex, so "transient" is an across-group inference; the 48-hour washout means tissue-level flatness is expected for an acute metabolite; and absolute concentrations, and therefore whether tissue Lac-Phe reaches physiologically meaningful levels, are not established. The organ source–sink assignments draw on mouse genetic models and are assumed, not independently shown, to be conserved in rat. What the analysis does establish is a recovered, chemically credible, body-wide signal; a training response bounded honestly to one tissue; a made-on-demand biosynthetic logic; and a specific, testable agenda — standard-confirmed MS/MS quantitation, acute rather than 48-hour sampling, and upstream-receptor deorphanization — that is the necessary bridge from an exercise metabolite hiding in plain sight to a Lac-Phe-based exercise mimetic.

content.text: The install is binaries-only (no tlpkg perl tree). I can bypass `fmtutil`/perl entirely and build the format directly with `pdftex -ini`.

input.human_description: Building pdflatex format via pdftex ini
input.command:
ENV=/Users/sgh2449/.claude-science/conda/envs/python
export PATH="$ENV/bin:$PATH"
export TEXMFVAR="/tmp/texmf-var"; mkdir -p "$TEXMFVAR/web2c/pdftex"
find $ENV -name "pdflatex.ini" 2>/dev/null | head
echo "--- building fmt ---"
cd "$TEXMFVAR/web2c/pdftex"
pdftex -ini -etex "pdflatex.ini" 2>&1 | tail -15
echo "EXIT ${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
ls -la *.fmt 2>&1
