## Source reading (structure survey)
Read the `.tex` Results §2 (L98–300), Discussion/Methods/refs tail (L300–595, dumped to `handoff/tex_tail.txt`). Confirmed section landmarks: Abstract L28, Intro L63, Results L98, Discussion L355, Methods L463, Data/Code availability L510, refs enumerate L550–589 (38 `\item`), `\end{document}` L594; total 595 lines. Introduction (L63–97) read in full — cites [Li 2022], [Jansen 2015], [Xiao 2024a; Scott 2024], [Xiao 2024b], [Amar 2024; Schenk 2024]; no Hedaya/Ying in-text.

**Retracted-ref check:** grep initially reported "Binary file matches" for Hedaya/Ying because the `.tex` had null bytes; text-mode grep confirmed **NONE** present in reference list or in-text. The null bytes were 10 chars in 4 figure-caption title placeholders (`\textit{\x000\x00} \textit{...}` at L144/148/181/300) plus a tail metadata placeholder (`is in 0.` at the Data-availability line, was `\x00`→rendered).

## Content edits applied (all via python string-replace on `cndp2_paper_work.tex`)
Read three new-content source files first: `build2/drug_design_text.md` (Fig5 caption + ~250w methods; parent PubChem CID 11075454, CAS 183241-73-8, no ChEMBL record, 12 analogs), `build2/strengthened_discussion.md` (16661 chars converted; 8 new keys), `build2/cndp2_markers_update.md` (strand-corrected §2.3 + Tables), `build2/references_delta.bib`, `build2/lacphe_analogs_admet.csv`.

Sequence of edits (each confirmed by growing file length; final ~65KB):
1. **Author block** — replaced "Automated multi-omic analysis pipeline" + affil with `\author[1]{Steven Hershman}` `\author[2]{Claude Science}` `\affil[1]{Independent Researcher}` `\affil[2]{Anthropic}`.
2. **Null bytes** — stripped all (`tex.replace("\x00","")`); replaced 4 empty `\textit{0}` caption tokens with proper bold Figure titles (Fig1–4); fixed tail `is in 0.}` → `is in \texttt{lacphe\_literature\_refs.csv}.}`.

input.human_description: Inserting spectrum figure float and reference
input.code:
tex=open("build3/cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex").read()

# Insert new Figure 2 float right after Figure 1's includegraphics line
fig1_inc=r"\begin{figure}[H]\centering\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/figure1_organ_timecourse.png}\end{figure}"
assert tex.count(fig1_inc)==1

fig2_block = fig1_inc + "\n\n" + r"""\begin{figure}[H]\centering\includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{figures/figure1c_spectrum.png}\end{figure}

\textbf{Figure 2. The raw negative-mode spectrum at the Lac-Phe apex resolves the intact ion, its diagnostic in-source fragment, and a built-in negative control.} \textit{Centroided MS1 spectrum ($m/z$ 80--250) at the validated Lac-Phe retention time (3.18 min) in a representative trained sample. The intact deprotonated molecule [M$-$H]$^-$ at $m/z$ 236.093 (red) and its in-source fragment [Phe$-$H]$^-$ at $m/z$ 164.072 (blue; neutral loss of the lactoyl C\textsubscript{3}H\textsubscript{4}O\textsubscript{2} moiety) co-elute and co-vary across the 50 biological samples (Spearman r = 0.96, p = 4$\times$10\textsuperscript{$-$27}), the parent--fragment relationship a coincidental isobar cannot reproduce. Free lactate [Lac$-$H]$^-$ at $m/z$ 89.024 (grey), the other half of the molecule, serves as a built-in negative control: it is swamped by the large free-lactate pool and does not track the intact ion. These MS1 relationships, together with the retention-time--hydrophobicity ordering, the CNDP2-family co-variation and the isotope envelope, place the assignment at MSI Level 2; no MS/MS is available in the deposit (all scans are MS1) and Lac-Phe is absent from public spectral libraries.}"""

tex=tex.replace(fig1_inc, fig2_block)

open("build3/cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex","w").write(tex)

content.content:
{"stdout":"\section*{Abstract}

N-lactoyl-phenylalanine (Lac-Phe) is an exercise-inducible, CNDP2-derived metabolite
that suppresses feeding and adiposity, yet it is not annotated in any released MoTrPAC
metabolomics feature table. Returning to the raw Agilent and Thermo mass-spectrometry
files, we recovered an ion at Lac-Phe's exact mass across all 19 MoTrPAC PASS1B rat
tissues and characterised its training response. A positive-control benchmark --- running
the identical extraction on twelve metabolites MoTrPAC did release --- reproduces their
released mass (11/12 within $\pm$5 ppm), retention time (11/12 within $\Delta$RT 0.03 min) and
training dose-response ($\Delta$log\textsubscript{2}FC concordance r = 0.75 across the eleven reliably-detected
metabolites), confirming the bespoke pipeline is quantitatively faithful for Lac-Phe's
chemical class. The response is transient --- peaking at
1--4 weeks of endurance training and decaying toward baseline by week 8 --- female-
predominant, concordant across ionization modes in plasma, heart, white adipose and
liver, and --- on a selection-free timecourse omnibus --- statistically robust in only one
compartment, the heart (q = 0.014); no tissue survives the stricter selection-aware
correction. We then asked whether the enzyme that makes Lac-Phe, CNDP2, is itself \textit{activated}
by training at any regulatory layer that protein abundance cannot see. Across MoTrPAC's
epigenetic and proteomic assays, it is not: 0 of 26 chromatin-accessibility peaks and
0 of 125 DNA-methylation clusters at the CNDP2 locus --- whose strand-aware coverage extends
across the promoter and 5$'$ upstream region to $\approx$15 kb, including a promoter CpG island
--- reach significance in any of 8 tissues; total CNDP2 protein is flat in 6 of 7 tissues; and no phosphorylation,
ubiquitylation or acetylation site shows an activating change. The only significant
protein/PTM effects are two hepatic \textit{decreases} (total protein and acetyl-K364), which
point away from enzyme induction. This convergent absence of activation is not a
failure of the assays --- the same tissues yield thousands of training-responsive
epigenetic features genome-wide --- but a positive mechanistic result: it corroborates a
model, built here from 46 verified primary sources, in which Lac-Phe production is
\textbf{substrate-driven flux} gated by local lactate and phenylalanine, not by CNDP2
induction. Under that source--sink model, the transient time course reflects the acute
lactate response and its attenuation by training adaptation, and the tissue restriction
reflects where circulating signal and local flux converge. We use this to frame what a Lac-Phe-raising exercise-mimetic would have to do, and --- because
the substrate-driven logic makes molecular persistence, not enzyme expression, the tractable
lever --- present an in-silico panel of hydrolysis-resistant Lac-Phe analogs and triage seven
through a five-stage computational funnel (quantum-chemical stability, pharmacophore alignment,
ADMET, target-agnostic docking, and physics-based co-fold), nominating a 1,2,3-triazole
isostere as lead. Identity here is at
","stderr":"","exit_code":0}

content.text: Now Tier 2: SLC17A1/3 explicit "not training-regulated" and the 48h timing interpretation in Discussion 3.2. Let me read §3.2.

input.human_description: Reading discussion 3.2 for SLC17 and timing
input.command: cd build3; sed -n '544,560p' cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex

content.content:
{"stdout":"
This places Lac-Phe in an instructive position within the exerkine landscape. Many mediators
of the exercise response are induced factors --- secreted proteins and peptides whose
transcription or release rises with training [Chow 2022] --- and it would have been reasonable
to expect a metabolite messenger to be governed the same way, through the abundance of its
maker. Lac-Phe is not: it belongs with the class of exercise signals set by metabolic flux,
of which lactate itself is the archetype [Brooks 2023]. The distinction is not semantic. It
is precisely why "substrate, not switch" is the correct verb for this system, and why a
snapshot of enzyme abundance --- the readout most atlases would reach for --- is the wrong
instrument to detect a Lac-Phe response. We are careful not to over-read the mechanism from
a single cross-sectional atlas: the enzyme-flat result bounds the \textit{direction} of regulation,
not the absolute flux, and identity here rests at MSI Level 2. But the direction is
unambiguous, and it is the direction the founding biochemistry predicts.

\subsection*{3.2 An organ source--sink model explains the time courses}

Placing the cross-tissue data against the primary literature yields a coherent organ-level
","stderr":"","exit_code":0}

input.command: cd build3; sed -n '560,600p' cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex

content.content:
{"stdout":"Placing the cross-tissue data against the primary literature yields a coherent organ-level
picture (Figure 1b). Lac-Phe is a blood-borne metabolite with distributed synthesis and
specialised clearance. It is made wherever CNDP2 meets lactate and phenylalanine ---
principally the intestinal epithelium at rest and after metformin [Xiao 2024a; TeSlaa 2024;
Ghias 2025], and contracting skeletal muscle and its resident immune cells during exercise
[Li 2022; Everaert 2012] --- exported to a common plasma pool, and excreted by the kidney
through the SLC17A1/3 transporters, which decouple the urine pool from the blood pool such
that transporter knockout lowers urine but not plasma Lac-Phe [Li 2024]. The brain is where
the circulating signal \textit{acts}, directly inhibiting hypothalamic AgRP neurons via
K\textsubscript{ATP}-channel activation to drive hypophagia [Liu 2025] --- a receptor/effector site rather
than a large metabolic pool. Adipose is positioned as a downstream responder whose
remodeling the circulating signal predicts, the pivotal human observation being that
post-exercise plasma Lac-Phe forecasts abdominal adipose loss over an endurance program
[Hoene 2022].

This model accounts for each feature of our data, and does so quantitatively where the
literature allows. Ubiquitous detectability follows directly from a circulating analyte
distributed to every perfused tissue: the shared plasma pool guarantees a measurable ion in
all 19 tissues without requiring autonomous local production. A tissue-restricted training
\textit{effect}, by contrast, requires a compartment where local substrate flux changes enough to
lift the local pool above that plasma background. The heart is the strongest candidate on
first principles --- continuously perfused, an avid lactate consumer, and subject to a
sustained workload increase with training --- and it is precisely the tissue where the effect
survives the selection-free omnibus (q = 0.014), with plasma and white adipose concordant
across ionization modes but suggestive only. This is not merely a post-hoc rationalisation
of which tissue "won": an orthogonal multi-tissue integration of the same PASS1B animals
places heart within a tightly coupled visceral/secretory organ module (heart--kidney,
heart--liver, heart--lung all co-varying within individual animals after the shared training
timeline is removed) whose members are the high-perfusion, high-substrate-flux organs the
source--sink model flags as most tightly coupled to the circulating pool. The compartment
first-principles nominate as the Lac-Phe integrator is the same one an unsupervised
cross-tissue analysis identifies as a coordinated metabolic hub.

The transient time course follows from mass action under training adaptation. Endurance
training lowers the blood-lactate response to a fixed workload, so a substrate-driven signal
is largest early --- in na\"\i{}ve animals, at high relative intensity and high lactate --- and
shrinks as the lactate response adapts; read across a cross-sectional weeks-1/2/4/8 design,
that is an early rise decaying by week 8. The human data are consistent on both the acute
and the chronic limb: circulating Lac-Phe scales with exercise intensity in a randomized
crossover trial, tracking the lactate load [Weber 2025], and within-subject longitudinal
sampling shows the acute per-session rise attenuating numerically over a training program,
","stderr":"","exit_code":0}

content.text: §3.2 already describes SLC17A1/3 clearance and decoupling well. It's missing the explicit "these transporters are not themselves training-regulated" point and the sharpened 48h sampling interpretation. Let me read a bit more and find the best insertion point.

input.command: cd build3; sed -n '600,615p' cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex

content.content:
{"stdout":"sampling shows the acute per-session rise attenuating numerically over a training program,
albeit as a non-significant trend in an exploratory cohort [Glatz 2025]. The female
predominance is, honestly, thinly supported externally --- one study reports a sex-specific
acute Lac-Phe response [Bauhaus 2025], while other N-lactoyl-amino-acid work is single-sex
or does not analyse sex --- so we treat it as an empirical finding to be explained within the
substrate/clearance framework (sex differences in phenylalanine availability, in the
relative-intensity lactate response at matched absolute workload, or in renal clearance all
sit upstream of CNDP2) rather than as established biology.

Two structural caveats bound the model. The PASS1B design is cross-sectional, so "transient"
is an across-group inference, not a within-animal trajectory; the closest longitudinal
analogue, the within-subject attenuation reported by [Glatz 2025], is in humans and does not
reach significance. And much of the organ-role evidence --- gut source, kidney clearance, AgRP
site of action --- is from mouse genetic models assumed, but not here shown, to be conserved in
rat. The source--sink model is a framework for interpreting the time courses, not a claim
that each rat tissue has been directly assigned a source or sink role.
","stderr":"","exit_code":0}

content.text: Now I'll add the SLC17-not-regulated point (to the clearance sentence) and the 48h sampling interpretation (to the transient paragraph). Both plug in cleanly.

input.code:
tex=open("build3/cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex").read()

# 1. SLC17 not-regulated: extend the clearance sentence
old1=r"""through the SLC17A1/3 transporters, which decouple the urine pool from the blood pool such
that transporter knockout lowers urine but not plasma Lac-Phe [Li 2024]."""
new1=r"""through the SLC17A1/3 transporters, which decouple the urine pool from the blood pool such
that transporter knockout lowers urine but not plasma Lac-Phe [Li 2024]. In PASS1B kidney,
neither \textit{Slc17a1} nor \textit{Slc17a3} is training-regulated at transcript or protein
(all training contrasts non-significant), so renal clearance capacity is a constitutive term:
the plasma pool is set by production, not by induced transport, which is consistent with
kidney reading as a training-associated \textit{decrease} (clearance of a rising pool) rather
than a source."""
assert tex.count(old1)==1
tex=tex.replace(old1,new1)

# 2. 48h sampling interpretation — add before "Two structural caveats"
old2=r"""Two structural caveats bound the model. The PASS1B design is cross-sectional,"""
new2=r"""A design feature of the atlas sharpens this reading. PASS1B collected every tissue
$\approx$48 h after the last exercise bout, a deliberate washout that captures a
trained-recovered state rather than the acute post-exercise window in which plasma Lac-Phe
spikes most steeply [Li 2022]. For an acute, substrate-driven metabolite this timing predicts
exactly what we see --- most tissues flat, the surviving signal a modest chronic-adaptation
residual well below the acute excursion --- and it explains why the feature never rose above
MoTrPAC's peak-picking threshold in the first place: the atlas was, by design, sampling away
from Lac-Phe's peak.

Two structural caveats bound the model. The PASS1B design is cross-sectional,"""
assert tex.count(old2)==1
tex=tex.replace(old2,new2)
open("build3/cndp2_lacphe_substrate_not_switch.tex","w").write(tex)

content.text:
Done. I addressed the review advice against Paper B, and walked back the Boltz-2 references since those results aren't coming.

## What I changed

**Boltz-2 walk-back** (results not arriving) — abstract, §2.5, and Methods now state plainly that the co-fold inputs were *prepared/generated* for wet-lab follow-up but **not run**, and are **not folded into the ranking**, which rests entirely on the four executed stages. Removed the "five-stage funnel" framing that implied completion.

**Tier 1**
1. **Compute-orchestration narrative** — new Methods "Compute orchestration" paragraph naming the heterogeneous split (local CPU for all Agilent decoding and statistics; ephemeral Modal Linux containers only for the x86-only Thermo vendor-reader step) and the ZIP64 HTTP-range trick that avoided multi-GB downloads. Added a one-line version to the abstract. (I described the local machine accurately as "a macOS workstation, 14 cores, no discrete GPU" — I didn't assert a specific chip model I can't confirm.)
2. **Annotated raw-spectrum panel** — new **Figure 2**, built from the real per-sample MS1 spectra: the intact [M−H]⁻ (236.093), the co-eluting in-source fragment [Phe−H]⁻ (164.072, r=0.96), and the built-in negative control [Lac−H]⁻ (89.024) that does *not* co-vary. Downstream figures renumbered 2→3…5→6.
3. **Second fragment statistic (ρ=0.83)** — **not added**: that value is from Paper B and isn't in our artifacts. I won't fabricate it.
4. **Tyrosine reframed as a finding** — now presented as catching a MoTrPAC mis-annotation (released *m/z* 180.0578 is ≈49 ppm below the true [M−H]⁻ of 180.0666), not "weak ionization."

**Tier 2** — added the ≈48 h post-bout washout interpretation (atlas captures a trained-recovered state, explaining why most tissues are flat and why the feature fell below the peak-picking threshold) to Discussion 3.2, and the explicit **SLC17A1/3-not-training-regulated → clearance is constitutive** point.

**Tier 3 (repo link)** — replaced the misleading fake `github.com/USERNAME/REPO` with an honest bracketed placeholder. **This one needs you**: only you can create/post the real repository. Confirmed. The source conversation computed exactly **one** in-source fragment covariation — the 164.072 ([Phe−H]⁻) at r=0.956, p=4×10⁻²⁷, already in the paper. content.text:
I've traced it thoroughly. Here's the answer.

## No — that conversation doesn't provide a real ρ=0.83 second-fragment statistic

I searched the full 3.3 MB source conversation. The `0.83` values in it are **CNDP2-family member correlations**, not an in-source fragment statistic:
- Lac-Trp r=0.835, Lac-Met r=0.825, Lac-Leu/Ile r=0.799, Lac-Tyr r=0.696 — cross-sample covariation of Lac-Phe with *other N-lactoyl amino acids*. That's the "family" evidence line, already in the paper as one of the four MS1 lines.

On in-source fragments, the conversation computed **exactly one** covariation, the same one already in the manuscript:
- `insource_frag_mz=164.0717, frag_intact_r=0.956, frag_p=4e-27` (the [Phe−H]⁻ fragment) — plus the free-Phe negative control `freephe_frag_r=-0.10`.

The `147.0452` "phenylpyruvate-like" mass (the [Phe−H−NH₃]⁻-type ion the advice refers to) appears only in a **list of expected/reference fragments** — it was never correlated against the intact ion. There is no ρ=0.83, no p=7×10⁻¹⁴, and no second-fragment analysis anywhere in the conversation.

## Could I compute it now?

No — not from available data. The real per-sample fragment intensities live in the raw-spectra extraction, which the conversation's own audit explicitly notes are "raw per-sample fragment traces, not in the supplied CSVs." The one fragment artifact I have (`fragment_spectra_pair.json`) holds just two representative spectra (one control, one trained), not the n=50 series a correlation needs. So computing a genuine 147-vs-236 ρ isn't possible here either.

## What this means for the paper

I'm leaving the manuscript unchanged. Adding a ρ=0.83 second-fragment statistic would still be fabrication — the source you shared confirms it was never computed; the 0.83 there is a different quantity (family co-variation) that the paper already reports correctly. The identity section stands honestly on the four MS1 lines it has, including the single verified in-source fragment (164.072, r=0.96).

If you want that second fragment as an actual evidence line, the path is to re-run the raw-spectra extraction to pull per-sample 147.045 intensities across the 50 plasma files and correlate them against the intact 236.093 — that's a real analysis I can set up, but it needs the raw-data pipeline, not the summary artifacts. Want me to do that?
